★ Scripture Studio

Biblical Timeline

Walk through the history of Scripture — creation to the early church

🕐 Biblical Timeline

■ Primeval History ■ Patriarchs ■ Israel & Kings ■ Prophets & Exile ■ Life of Christ ■ Early Church
Primeval History
Creation
In the Beginning
God creates the heavens, earth, and humanity in six days. Adam and Eve placed in the Garden of Eden.
Genesis 1–2
The Fall
Sin Enters the World
Adam and Eve disobey God. Sin and death enter creation. The first promise of a Redeemer given.
Genesis 3  ·  Protoevangelium (Gen. 3:15)
The Great Flood
~2348 BC
God judges the earth by flood. Noah and his family preserved. The rainbow covenant established.
Genesis 6–9
Patriarchal Era
Call of Abraham
~2091 BC
God calls Abram from Ur. The Abrahamic Covenant: land, seed, and blessing to all nations.
Genesis 12  ·  Romans 4
Isaac, Jacob & Joseph
~2066–1806 BC
The covenant line passes through Isaac and Jacob (Israel). Joseph sold into Egypt — God's sovereign plan unfolds.
Genesis 21–50
Israel & the Kingdom
The Exodus
~1446 BC
Moses leads Israel out of Egyptian bondage. The Ten Plagues, Passover, and crossing of the Red Sea.
Exodus 1–15  ·  Hebrews 11:29
The Law at Sinai
~1446 BC
God gives Moses the Ten Commandments and the Mosaic Covenant. The Tabernacle constructed for worship.
Exodus 19–40
Conquest of Canaan
~1406 BC
Joshua leads Israel into the Promised Land. The fall of Jericho. Tribal territories distributed.
Joshua 1–24
Kingdom of David & Solomon
~1010–931 BC
David unites Israel and establishes Jerusalem. Solomon builds the Temple. The Davidic Covenant promises an eternal throne.
2 Samuel 7  ·  1 Kings 6
Prophets & Exile
Division of the Kingdom
~931 BC
Israel splits into Northern (Israel) and Southern (Judah) kingdoms. A succession of mostly wicked kings follows.
1 Kings 12
Babylonian Exile
~605–538 BC
Nebuchadnezzar destroys Jerusalem and the Temple. Judah taken captive to Babylon. Daniel and Ezekiel minister in exile.
2 Kings 25  ·  Daniel 1
The Return & Restoration
~538–430 BC
Cyrus of Persia decrees Israel's return. Ezra restores the Law; Nehemiah rebuilds Jerusalem's walls. Malachi is the last OT prophet.
Ezra 1  ·  Nehemiah  ·  Malachi
Life of Christ
Birth of Jesus
~4 BC  ·  Bethlehem
The Word made flesh. Born of a virgin in Bethlehem. Announced by angels, visited by shepherds and magi.
Matthew 1–2  ·  Luke 2  ·  John 1:14
Ministry of Jesus
~AD 27–30  ·  Galilee & Judea
Three years of teaching, healing, and miracles. The Sermon on the Mount, parables, and calling of the twelve disciples.
Matthew 5–7  ·  Mark  ·  Luke  ·  John
The Crucifixion
~AD 30  ·  Golgotha, Jerusalem
Jesus is betrayed, tried, crucified, and buried. The veil of the Temple torn in two. Redemption accomplished.
Matthew 27  ·  Romans 5:8  ·  Isaiah 53
The Resurrection
~AD 30  ·  The Empty Tomb
Jesus rises bodily from the dead on the third day. He appears to more than 500 witnesses. The foundation of Christian faith.
Matthew 28  ·  1 Corinthians 15:3–8
Early Church
Day of Pentecost
~AD 30  ·  Jerusalem
The Holy Spirit descends on 120 disciples. Peter preaches; 3,000 saved. The Church is born.
Acts 2
Paul's Missionary Journeys
~AD 46–60
Three major missionary journeys across Asia Minor and Europe. Churches established in Corinth, Philippi, Ephesus, and beyond.
Acts 13–28
Revelation & The Promise
~AD 95  ·  Isle of Patmos
John receives visions of the risen Christ, the tribulation, and the new creation. Jesus declares: "I am coming soon."
Revelation 1  ·  Revelation 22:20

Bishop's Commentary

Verse-by-verse expository insight from Bishop Richard G. Clark — deep, pastoral & Scripture-rooted

Gathering voices from the scholars...
📖

Type a scripture reference above — choose your lens — and click Generate Commentary to see what Bishop Clark and the great commentators have said about that text.

Bishop R.G. Clark Matthew Henry Jamieson, Fausset & Brown Albert Barnes Adam Clarke
◆ Commentary Panel

Scripture

Five voices. One text. Every perspective unique.

☀ Today’s Recommended Reading
Psalm 119 — The Word of God

The longest chapter in the Bible is entirely devoted to the glory of God’s Word. Every one of its 176 verses refers to Scripture. The author — likely a young Levite in exile — found that when everything else was stripped away, God’s Word remained. That is the testimony every serious student of Scripture eventually arrives at: the Bible is not just a book to study, it is a lifeline to hold.

📖
Romans 8
No condemnation — no separation
🌿
John 15
Abiding and bearing fruit
🦅
Isaiah 40
Renew your strength
✌️
Philippians 4
Peace that passes understanding
🛡️
Hebrews 11
Hall of Faith
🌅
Revelation 21
All things new

Select any book of the Bible for Bishop Clark’s overview — theme, key passages, and how it points to Christ.

📖
Scripture Interprets Scripture

The clearest interpreter of any passage is another passage. Before reading what a commentator says, always ask: what does the rest of the Bible say about this? Cross-reference, compare, and let God’s Word be its own best teacher.

✝️
Christ-Centered Hermeneutic

Jesus said all Scripture testifies about Him (John 5:39). Every passage — law, history, poetry, prophecy — finds its ultimate meaning in the person and work of Christ. Ask of every text: where is Jesus here?

🔍
Observe, Interpret, Apply

The three-step method: What does it say? (Observation) What does it mean? (Interpretation) What should I do? (Application). Never jump to application without first understanding what the text actually says and means in its original context.

📜
Context is King

A text without a context is a pretext. Read every verse in light of its surrounding verses, its chapter, its book, its testament, and the whole canon. The most common error in Bible study is lifting a verse from its context and building a doctrine on air.

🌍
Historical-Cultural Grounding

The Bible was written in specific times, places, and cultures. Understanding the customs, geography, political climate, and language of the original audience transforms how you read every passage. Truth is eternal — but it was first spoken into a particular moment in history.

🙏
Spirit-Illuminated Study

The same Spirit who inspired the Word must illuminate it for the reader. Study is not just a mental exercise — it is a spiritual discipline. The Holy Spirit guides believers into all truth (John 16:13). Begin every study session with prayer. The text opens differently when your heart is open first.
